arguments.command: which llvm-symbolizer llvm-addr2line addr2line aarch64-linux-gnu-addr2line 2>/dev/null; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5

data.reasoningText:
Now I need to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames. The key libraries are:

1. `libmonosgen-2.0.so` (BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be) - frames #01, #02, #03, #06
2. `libSystem.Native.so` (BuildId: 7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213) - frames #04, #07


3. `libSystem.Globalization.Native.so` (BuildId: 2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e) - frame #05

To resolve these addresses, I'll need to fetch the debug symbols for each library using their Build IDs from Microsoft's symbol server, then use tools like `addr2line` or `llvm-symbolizer` to map the addresses to actual function names and source locations.

For .NET runtime native libraries on ARM64 Android, I can either use the `dotnet-symbol` tool to automatically download symbols, leverage the debuginfod protocol, or manually retrieve them from Microsoft's symbol servers using the Build ID format. I'm working through the URL formats for Microsoft's symbol server to retrieve the ELF debug symbols, trying different endpoint patterns for both the library files and their associated debug info. Let me check what tools are available on the system first—like llvm-symbolizer or addr2line—then attempt to download the symbol files from the server to help with symbolicating the .NET runtime crash on Android. I'll start by checking what tools are available on the system and attempting to download the symbols from the server.

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols && cd /tmp/symbols

# Try downloading debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server for each library
# Format: https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>

# libmonosgen-2.0.so (BuildId: 1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be)
echo "=== Downloading libmonosgen-2.0.so debug info ==="
curl -sSL -o mono.debug "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/_.debug" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1
curl -sSL -o libmonosgen-2.0.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be/libmonosgen-2.0.so" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1

echo ""
echo "=== Downloading libSystem.Native.so debug info ==="
curl -sSL -o native.debug "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213/_.debug" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1
curl -sSL -o libSystem.Native.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Native.so/elf-buildid-7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213/libSystem.Native.so" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1

echo ""
echo "=== Downloading libSystem.Globalization.Native.so debug info ==="
curl -sSL -o glob.debug "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e/_.debug" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1
curl -sSL -o libSystem.Globalization.Native.so "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so/elf-buildid-2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e/libSystem.Globalization.Native.so" -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" 2>&1

echo ""
ls -la /tmp/symbols/
arguments.description: Download debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server

data.reasoningText:
I have `addr2line` available. Since these are arm64 (aarch64) binaries but I'm on an amd64 system, `addr2line` should still work with the ELF files since it just reads DWARF debug info — it doesn't execute the binaries. Actually, `addr2line` from GNU binutils can handle cross-architecture ELF files.

Let me try to download the symbol files from Microsoft's symbol server. I need to:

1. Download the .so files (or .dbg files) for each library
2. Use addr2line to resolve the addresses

Let me try downloading from the Microsoft symbol server. The format for ELF debug symbols is:


For the library: `https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>`

For debug symbols: `https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-<buildid>/_.debug`

Let me try downloading the debug symbols for each library using the MSDL symbol server URL format where I construct the path with the filename and buildid.

Now I'm looking at the build IDs more carefully—the first one for libmonosgen-2.0.so appears to be a legitimate SHA1 hash, but the second one for libSystem.Native.so seems incomplete or truncated.

The third build ID for libSystem.Globalization.Native.so has a very repetitive pattern that looks artificially constructed rather than genuine. I should attempt to download these symbols anyway using the Microsoft symbol server, or try alternative tools like symchk or dotnet-symbol if they're available.

arguments.command:
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols && cd /tmp/symbols

# Try downloading debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server for each library
# Format: https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/<filename>/elf-buildid-<buildid>/<filename>

data.reasoningText:
I got the debug symbols for libmonosgen-2.0.so (both the library and debug info downloaded successfully). The other two libraries (libSystem.Native.so and libSystem.Globalization.Native.so) returned 404, which means those specific build IDs aren't on the symbol server - they might be fabricated test data.

Let me now use addr2line to symbolicate the monosgen frames. I have:
- Frame #01: pc 0x222098
- Frame #02: pc 0x222234
- Frame #03: pc 0x19bac0
- Frame #06: pc 0x19bc14


Now I'm verifying that the downloaded files are intact and usable.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have the symbolicated frames for libmonosgen-2.0.so. The other two libraries (libSystem.Native.so and libSystem.Globalization.Native.so) couldn't be found on the symbol server — likely because those build IDs are fabricated for this test file.

Let me also try alternate symbol server URLs for those two, or check if they might be under different paths.

Actually, looking at the build IDs again:
- libSystem.Native.so: 7a3b5c9d1e4f6082a3c5d7e9f1b2d4a6c8e0f213


- libSystem.Globalization.Native.so: 2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e2f4a6b8c0d2e4f6a8c0e

The Globalization one has that very patterned, repeating structure which screams synthetic. Let me try some alternate symbol server URLs — maybe the dotnet symbol server at `https://symbols.nuget.org/download/symbols/` or `https://dotnetcli.blob.core.windows.net/dotnet/symbols/`.

data.reasoningText:
The symbols for `libSystem.Native.so` and `libSystem.Globalization.Native.so` aren't available on any symbol server I tried. These build IDs are likely fabricated/synthetic for this test tombstone file. Let me present the results I have.

Let me now clean up and present the symbolicated backtrace.

data.content:
Here's the symbolicated backtrace. I was able to resolve the 4 `libmonosgen-2.0.so` frames using debug symbols from Microsoft's symbol server. The other two libraries (`libSystem.Native.so`, `libSystem.Globalization.Native.so`) had build IDs not found on any symbol server.

```
#00 pc 0x6ae1c   libc.so                            abort+164
#01 pc 0x222098  libmonosgen-2.0.so                  ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast (icall.c:6244)
#02 pc 0x222234  libmonosgen-2.0.so                  ves_icall_System_ArgIterator_Setup (icall.c:6353)
#03 pc 0x19bac0  libmonosgen-2.0.so                  get_saved_signal_handler (mini-posix.c:140)
#04 pc 0x8f14    libSystem.Native.so                 (symbols not available)
#05 pc 0x3b20    libSystem.Globalization.Native.so   (symbols not available)
#06 pc 0x19bc14  libmonosgen-2.0.so                  sampling_thread_func (mini-posix.c:640)
                                                      ↳ inlined: mono_chain_signal (mini-posix.c:197)
                                                      ↳ inlined: profiler_signal_handler (mini-posix.c:309)
#07 pc 0x12a48   libSystem.Native.so                 (symbols not available)
```

The crash originates from a `SIGSEGV` (null pointer dereference at `0x0`). The call chain shows `Environment.FailFast` was invoked through the Mono runtime's signal handling path — a signal handler caught the `SIGSEGV` and triggered a managed fail-fast abort.
